articles.full_text:
Ground ReportSariska tourism halves amid scorching heat:Alwar's famed milk cake sales dip, women queue for water as mercury touches 46°C
- Copy link
'Nautapa' entered its seventh day on Sunday. Although recent storms and rain brought some relief, residents of Alwar still recall the intense heat of the past week. The city, like much of Rajasthan, witnessed scorching conditions, with the maximum temperature reaching 46°C.
By 9 am, the sun was already intense, while hot winds (loo) during the afternoon severely affected daily life. Roads and markets remained largely deserted during peak hours, giving the impression of an undeclared curfew across the city.
The extreme heat has also impacted Alwar's tourism and business sectors. The city is known for its famous milk cake (kalakand) and the Sariska Tiger Reserve, both of which have seen a sharp decline in visitors and customers.
The number of tourists visiting Sariska has dropped by nearly half, while kalakand sales have fallen by up to 50%. At the same time, many parts of the city are facing a worsening water crisis, with low water pressure and irregular supply forcing residents to depend on water tankers.
Women in several localities are spending hours in queues under the scorching sun to collect water.
Tourists halve at Sariska, guides seek shade
The intense heat has significantly reduced tourist footfall at Sariska Tiger Reserve.
According to reserve data, around 90,000 domestic and international tourists visit Sariska annually. The reserve usually receives about 800 visitors a day, but during Nautapa, daily tourist numbers have fallen to around 300-400.
The rocky hills of Sariska are also experiencing extreme temperatures. Safari guides, who usually remain busy throughout the day, are now often seen resting under trees to escape the heat.
Milk cake sales drop; furnaces run for fewer hours
Alwar's famous milk cake industry has also been affected by the soaring temperatures.
Shops that usually attract large crowds are seeing fewer customers. Sweet sellers say sales decline by nearly 40% during May and June, with demand hitting its lowest levels during Nautapa.
Confectioners report reduced workloads, and furnaces that once operated round the clock are now shut for five to six hours daily because of lower demand.
Green shade installed at traffic signals
To provide relief to commuters waiting at red lights, green shade structures have been installed at several traffic intersections across the city.
Water crisis worsens as temperature touches 46°C
The water shortage has become more severe amid the heatwave.
In several areas, residents receive water only once every three days. Women from Sonawa Dungri and nearby localities gather along the main road in the afternoon, waiting for water tankers to arrive.
Ramvati, a resident of Sonawa Dungri, said, "It feels like fire is raining from the sky. We spend the entire day waiting for water. Many times, we have to sit on the roadside throughout the afternoon."
Another resident, Changi Devi, said water is supplied only once in three days and even then the quantity is insufficient.
Shivram said the water shortage has become so severe that many households cannot even fill their coolers. "Very little water is coming through the taps. Tankers arrive in the afternoon, but often not on time, forcing people to wait in the heat," he said.
Relief after May 28 drizzle
Alwar received light drizzle on the evening of May 28. Rainfall was recorded later in the night, while some areas also witnessed hail.
Following the weather change, temperatures dropped by 3°C to 5°C. The district is expected to see maximum temperatures of around 42°C over the next few days, offering some respite from the severe heat.
